stem: Why should you normalise/scale features before training a k-nearest neighbours model?

options.text: KNN requires all features to be binary
Without scaling, features with larger numerical ranges dominate the distance calculation unfairly
Scaling reduces training time for KNN
KNN cannot handle missing values without scaling

explanation_after: KNN measures distance between points. If one feature ranges 0–100,000 (income) and another 0–10 (years experience), income will dominate the distance. Scaling puts all features on equal footing.

stem: You fit a StandardScaler on your training data. When preprocessing the test data, you should:

options.text: Fit a new StandardScaler on the test data
Transform the test data using the scaler already fitted on training data
Not scale the test data
Scale the test data with min-max normalisation instead

explanation_after: The scaler must be fit only on training data (to prevent leakage), then applied to both train and test using the same mean/std. Fitting on test data would leak test distribution information into preprocessing.

stem: One-hot encoding 'country' with 200 unique values creates:

options.text: 1 new column
2 new columns
200 new binary columns, one per country
A single numeric column mapping countries to numbers

explanation_after: One-hot encoding creates one binary column per unique category. For 200 countries, this creates 200 columns, each 0 or 1. This can be problematic for high-cardinality features.

stem: A sklearn Pipeline wrapping preprocessing and a classifier ensures:

options.text: Faster training
That the preprocessor is fit only on training data in each CV fold, preventing data leakage
That the classifier uses more features
That data augmentation is applied automatically

explanation_after: Without a Pipeline, if you fit the scaler on all data before cross-validation, test fold information leaks into preprocessing. A Pipeline fits and transforms within each CV fold correctly.

stem: Feature importance from a Random Forest tells you:

options.text: The causal effect of each feature on the target
Which features the tree used most to reduce impurity across all splits
The correlation coefficient between each feature and the target
Which features to remove before training

explanation_after: Tree-based feature importance measures how much each feature reduces impurity (Gini/entropy) across all splits. It indicates predictive usefulness but not causality — correlated features can share importance.

stem: What is 'training-serving skew' in ML feature engineering?

options.text: When the training set and test set have different class distributions
When features are computed differently in the training pipeline versus the production serving pipeline
When the model is too complex for the training data
When numerical features are not normalised

explanation_after: Training-serving skew occurs when features are computed with different logic in training versus production. For example, a '7-day average' computed with different window boundaries in training vs serving will make the model behave unexpectedly in production.
